seo.title: Security for AI Apps implementation checklist
seo.description: A practical checklist for shipping Security for AI Apps projects safely.

content.intro: This checklist outlines the technical verification steps required to secure LLM-integrated applications against prompt injection, data leakage, and infrastructure vulnerabilities before moving to a production environment.

items.title: Implement Delimiter Separation
items.description: Enclose user input within distinct delimiters (e.g., triple quotes or XML tags) in the prompt template to help the LLM distinguish between developer instructions and user-provided data.

items.title: Deploy an Injection Detection Layer
items.description: Integrate a dedicated security tool like Rebuff or a secondary 'judge' LLM to analyze incoming user prompts for known injection patterns before they reach the primary model.

items.title: Enforce Input Schema Validation
items.description: Use Zod or Pydantic to strictly validate the structure, type, and length of user inputs to prevent unexpected payloads from being processed by the LLM.

items.title: Apply Least-Privilege System Instructions
items.description: Audit the system prompt to ensure it explicitly instructs the model to ignore any user commands that attempt to override its core security constraints or access internal tools.

items.title: Test Against Indirect Injection
items.description: Verify that data fetched from third-party APIs or external websites is treated as untrusted user input and passed through the same sanitization pipeline as direct user prompts.

items.title: Implement Pre-Inference PII Scrubbing
items.description: Use a library like Microsoft Presidio or LLM Guard to identify and redact Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from user prompts before they are sent to external LLM providers.

items.title: Disable Provider Data Training
items.description: Verify in the LLM provider's settings (e.g., OpenAI Enterprise or Azure OpenAI) that user data is not utilized for model training or retained beyond the required retention period.

items.title: Audit Few-Shot Prompt Context
items.description: Scan the hardcoded examples in your prompt templates to ensure no production secrets, internal database schemas, or sensitive employee data are included as context.

items.title: Validate Data Localization Compliance
items.description: Confirm that the LLM provider's data center region aligns with your organization's residency requirements (e.g., GDPR data processing within the EU).

items.title: Encrypt Data in Transit and Rest
items.description: Ensure all communication with LLM APIs occurs over TLS 1.3 and that any locally cached prompt history is encrypted using AES-256.

items.title: Enable Native Moderation APIs
items.description: Pipe all LLM generated content through a moderation endpoint (e.g., OpenAI Moderation API) to block responses containing hate speech, self-harm, or illegal content.

items.title: Verify Output Structural Integrity
items.description: Use Guardrails AI or NeMo Guardrails to validate that the LLM output conforms to the expected JSON schema or code format before it is parsed by the application.

items.title: Filter Sensitive Information Leakage
items.description: Implement regex-based scanners on the LLM output to detect and block the accidental disclosure of internal IP addresses, API keys, or proprietary internal terminology.

items.title: Set Token Response Limits
items.description: Configure a strict 'max_tokens' parameter on all inference calls to prevent resource exhaustion and mitigate potential Denial of Wallet attacks via excessively long responses.

items.title: Sanitize Output for XSS
items.description: Apply standard HTML escaping to LLM-generated text before rendering it in a web UI to prevent the execution of malicious scripts injected via the model output.

items.title: Centralize API Key Storage
items.description: Move LLM provider API keys from environment variables into a managed secret store like AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or Azure Key Vault.

items.title: Implement User-Level Rate Limiting
items.description: Apply rate limits based on the authenticated user ID rather than IP address to prevent a single compromised account from draining your LLM API credits.

items.title: Rotate Production API Credentials
items.description: Establish an automated rotation policy for all LLM service keys and verify that legacy keys are immediately revoked upon rotation.

items.title: Use IAM Roles for Cloud LLMs
items.description: When using services like AWS Bedrock or Vertex AI, utilize IAM roles and service accounts instead of static access keys to follow the principle of least privilege.

items.title: Isolate AI Microservices
items.description: Deploy LLM-handling logic in an isolated network segment (VPC) with restricted egress to only the specific domains required by the LLM providers.

items.title: Log Inference Metadata
items.description: Record prompt/response pairs, token usage, and latency in a secure, centralized logging system (e.g., Datadog, ELK) while ensuring PII is masked in the logs.

items.title: Alert on High Refusal Rates
items.description: Configure monitoring alerts for when the LLM or moderation layer returns a high frequency of 'safety-related' refusals, which may indicate an active injection attempt.

items.title: Establish an Emergency Kill Switch
items.description: Implement a feature flag or configuration toggle that can instantly disable LLM functionality across the application without requiring a full code redeploy.

items.title: Perform Red-Teaming Exercises
items.description: Conduct a scheduled 'jailbreak' test where security engineers attempt to bypass current guardrails using techniques from the OWASP LLM Top 10.

items.title: Monitor Token Usage Anomalies
items.description: Set up automated alerts for token consumption spikes that exceed the 95th percentile of historical usage to detect automated abuse or recursive loop bugs.
